addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- Explore: Read-only search agent for broad fan-out searches — when answering means sweeping many files, directories, or naming conventions and you only need the conclusion, not the file dumps. It reads excerpts rather than whole files, so it locates code; it doesn't review or audit it. Specify search breadth: "medium" for moderate exploration, "very thorough" for multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

input.command: grep -n "ZOPFLI_APPEND_DATA\|#define ZOPFLI_APPEND_DATA" /app/Syzygy_Zopfli/c_code/zopfli.c | head; echo "---"; grep -rn "define ZOPFLI_APPEND_DATA" /app/Syzygy_Zopfli/c_code/

content.content:
200	whenever needed.
201	
202	value: the value to append, type T
203	data: pointer to the dynamic array to append to, type T**
204	size: pointer to the size of the array to append to, type size_t*. This is the
205	size that you consider the array to be, not the internal allocation size.
206	Precondition: allocated size of data is at least a power of two greater than or
207	equal than *size.
208	*/
209	#define ZOPFLI_APPEND_DATA(/* T */ value, /* T** */ data, /* size_t* */ size)        \
210	    {                                                                                \
211	        if (!((*size) & ((*size) - 1)))                                              \
212	        {                                                                            \
213	            /*double alloc size if it's a power of two*/                             \
214	            (*data) = (*size) == 0 ? malloc(sizeof(**data))                          \
215	                                   : realloc((*data), (*size) * 2 * sizeof(**data)); \
216	            memset((*data) + (*size), 0, (*size) * sizeof(**data));                  \
217	        }                                                                            \
218	        (*data)[(*size)] = (value);                                                  \
219	        (*size)++;                                                                   \
220	    }
221	
222	/*
223	Outputs minimum-redundancy length-limited code bitlengths for symbols with the
224	given counts. The bitlengths are limited by maxbits.
225	
226	The output is tailored for DEFLATE: symbols that never occur, get a bit length
227	of 0, and if only a single symbol occurs at least once, its bitlength will be 1,
228	and not 0 as would theoretically be needed for a single symbol.
229	
230	frequencies: The amount of occurrences of each symbol.
231	n: The amount of symbols.
232	maxbits: Maximum bit length, inclusive.
233	bitlengths: Output, the bitlengths for the symbol prefix codes.
234	return: 0 for OK, non-0 for error.

file.content:
whenever needed.

value: the value to append, type T
data: pointer to the dynamic array to append to, type T**
size: pointer to the size of the array to append to, type size_t*. This is the
size that you consider the array to be, not the internal allocation size.
Precondition: allocated size of data is at least a power of two greater than or
equal than *size.
*/
#define ZOPFLI_APPEND_DATA(/* T */ value, /* T** */ data, /* size_t* */ size)        \
    {                                                                                \
        if (!((*size) & ((*size) - 1)))                                              \
        {                                                                            \
            /*double alloc size if it's a power of two*/                             \
            (*data) = (*size) == 0 ? malloc(sizeof(**data))                          \
                                   : realloc((*data), (*size) * 2 * sizeof(**data)); \
            memset((*data) + (*size), 0, (*size) * sizeof(**data));                  \
        }                                                                            \
        (*data)[(*size)] = (value);                                                  \
        (*size)++;                                                                   \
    }

/*
Outputs minimum-redundancy length-limited code bitlengths for symbols with the
given counts. The bitlengths are limited by maxbits.

The output is tailored for DEFLATE: symbols that never occur, get a bit length
of 0, and if only a single symbol occurs at least once, its bitlength will be 1,
and not 0 as would theoretically be needed for a single symbol.

frequencies: The amount of occurrences of each symbol.
n: The amount of symbols.
maxbits: Maximum bit length, inclusive.
bitlengths: Output, the bitlengths for the symbol prefix codes.
return: 0 for OK, non-0 for error.

content.content:
1	# Frees Clauses
2	
3	A *frees clause* allows the user to specify a set of pointers that may be freed by a function or by the function it calls, transitively. A function contract may have zero or more frees clauses. When no clause is provided the empty set is used as default. Contracts can also have an empty frees clause. When more than one frees clause is given, the sets of pointers they contain are unioned together to yield a single set of pointers.
4	
5	## Syntax
6	
7	The clause has the following syntax (square brackets denote optional expressions `[` `]`):
8	
9	```
10	__CPROVER_frees([targets])
11	```
12	
13	Where `targets` has the following syntax:
14	
15	```
16	          targets ::= cond-target-group (';' cond-target-group)* ';'?
17	cond-target-group ::= (condition ':' )? target (',' target)*
18	           target ::= lvalue-expr
19	                    | __CPROVER_freeable(lvalue-expr)
20	```
21	
22	A frees clause target must be either:
23	
24	- an lvalue expression with a pointer type,
25	- a call to the built-in function `__CPROVER_freeable`
26	- a call to a user-defined side effect free and deterministic function returning the type `void` (itself containing direct or indirect calls to `__CPROVER_freeable` or to functions that call `__CPROVER_freeable`)
27	
28	### Example
29	
30	```c
31	int foo(char *arr1, char *arr2, size_t size)
32	__CPROVER_frees(
33	    // `arr1` is freeable only if the condition `size > 0 && arr1` holds
34	    size > 0 && arr1: arr1;
35	
36	    // `arr2` is always freeable
37	    arr2;
38	)
39	{
40	  if(size > 0 && arr1)
41	    free(arr1);
42	  free(arr2);
43	  return 0;
44	}
45	```
46	
47	## Semantics
48	
49	The set of pointers specified by the frees clause of the contract is interpreted at the function call-site where the contract is being checked or used to abstract a function call.
50	
51	### For contract checking
52	
53	When checking a contract against a function, each pointer that the function attempts to free gets checked for membership in the set of pointers specified by the *frees clause*.
54	
55	### For replacement of function calls by contracts
56	
57	When replacing a function call by a contract, each pointer of the *frees clause* gets non-deterministically freed between the evaluation of preconditions and before the evaluation of post-conditions.
58	
59	## Specifying parametric sets of freeable pointers using C functions
60	
61	Users can define parametric sets of freeable pointers by writing functions that return the `void` type and call (directly or indirectly) the built-in function `__CPROVER_freeable`:
62	
63	```c
64	void my_freeable_set(char **arr, size_t size)
65	{
66	  // The first 3 pointers are freeable
67	  // if the array is at least of size 3.
68	  if (arr && size > 3) {
69	    __CPROVER_freeable(arr[0]);
70	    __CPROVER_freeable(arr[1]);
71	    __CPROVER_freeable(arr[2]);
72	  }
73	}
74	```
75	
76	Calling the built-in function:
77	
78	```c
79	void __CPROVER_freeable(void *ptr);
80	```
81	
82	in the context of a frees clause specifies that `ptr` is freeable in that context.
83	
84	```c
85	void my_function(char **arr, size_t size)
86	__CPROVER_frees(
87	  // arr is considered freeable in the context of this clause.
88	  my_freeable_set(arr, size)
89	)
90	{
91	  // body ...
92	}
93	```
94	
95	## Frees clause related predicates
96	
97	The predicate:
98	
99	```c
100	__CPROVER_bool __CPROVER_is_freeable(void *ptr);
101	```
102	
103	can only be used in pre and post conditions, in contract checking or replacement modes. It returns `true` if and only if the pointer satisfies the preconditions of the `free` function from `stdlib.h`, that is if and only if the pointer points to a valid dynamically allocated object and has offset zero.
104	
105	The predicate:
106	
107	```c
108	__CPROVER_bool __CPROVER_was_freed(void *ptr);
109	```
110	
111	can only be used in post conditions and returns `true` if and only if the pointer was freed during the execution of the function under analysis.
112

content.content:
860	__CPROVER_requires(__CPROVER_is_fresh(out, sizeof(*out)))
861	/* *bp is a bit index within a byte, used as a shift amount. */
862	__CPROVER_requires(*bp <= 7)
863	/* Every caller emits a single Huffman code whose length is at most 7 bits here,
864	   so the bit cursor wraps to 0 at most once and thus at most a single fresh byte
865	   is appended over the whole loop. */
866	__CPROVER_requires(length <= 7)
867	/* The buffer holds at least one byte that can be updated, plus room to append
868	   one more byte without overflowing.  Constraining *outsize to a non-power-of-
869	   two (and non-zero) value keeps the ZOPFLI_APPEND_DATA macro on its
870	   no-reallocation path for that single append. */
871	__CPROVER_requires(((*outsize) & ((*outsize) - 1)) != 0)
872	__CPROVER_requires(__CPROVER_is_fresh(*out, (*outsize) + 1))
873	__CPROVER_assigns(*bp, *outsize, __CPROVER_object_whole(*out))
874	/* One fresh byte is appended for each time the bit cursor lands on 0 while
875	   emitting the `length` bits. */
876	__CPROVER_ensures(*outsize ==
877	                  __CPROVER_old(*outsize)
878	                      + ((__CPROVER_old(*bp) + length + 7) / 8
879	                         - (__CPROVER_old(*bp) + 7) / 8))
880	/* The bit cursor advances by `length`, modulo 8. */
881	__CPROVER_ensures(*bp == ((__CPROVER_old(*bp) + length) & 7))
882	{
883	    /* TODO(lode): make more efficient (add more bits at once). */
884	    unsigned i;
885	    for (i = 0; i < length; i++)
886	    {
887	        unsigned bit = (symbol >> (length - i - 1)) & 1;
888	        if (*bp == 0)
889	            ZOPFLI_APPEND_DATA(0, out, outsize);
890	        (*out)[*outsize - 1] |= bit << *bp;
891	        *bp = (*bp + 1) & 7;
892	    }
893	}
894	
895	static void AddBits(unsigned symbol, unsigned length,
896	                    unsigned char *bp, unsigned char **out, size_t *outsize)
897	/* bp, out, outsize must be valid pointers. */
898	__CPROVER_requires(__CPROVER_is_fresh(bp, sizeof(*bp)))
899	__CPROVER_requires(__CPROVER_is_fresh(outsize, sizeof(*outsize)))
900	__CPROVER_requires(__CPROVER_is_fresh(out, sizeof(*out)))
901	/* *bp is a bit index within a byte, used as a shift amount. */
902	__CPROVER_requires(*bp <= 7)
903	/* Every caller writes at most a 7-bit field (DEFLATE extra bits / fixed-width
904	   header fields), so the bit cursor wraps to 0 at most once and thus at most a
905	   single fresh byte is appended over the whole loop. */
906	__CPROVER_requires(length <= 7)
907	/* The buffer holds at least one byte that can be updated, plus room to append
908	   one more byte without overflowing.  Constraining *outsize to a non-power-of-
909	   two (and non-zero) value keeps the ZOPFLI_APPEND_DATA macro on its
910	   no-reallocation path for that single append. */
911	__CPROVER_requires(((*outsize) & ((*outsize) - 1)) != 0)
912	__CPROVER_requires(__CPROVER_is_fresh(*out, (*outsize) + 1))
913	__CPROVER_assigns(*bp, *outsize, __CPROVER_object_whole(*out))
914	/* One fresh byte is appended for each time the bit cursor lands on 0 while
915	   emitting the `length` bits. */
916	__CPROVER_ensures(*outsize ==
917	                  __CPROVER_old(*outsize)
918	                      + ((__CPROVER_old(*bp) + length + 7) / 8
919	                         - (__CPROVER_old(*bp) + 7) / 8))
920	/* The bit cursor advances by `length`, modulo 8. */
921	__CPROVER_ensures(*bp == ((__CPROVER_old(*bp) + length) & 7))
922	{
923	    /* TODO(lode): make more efficient (add more bits at once). */
924	    unsigned i;
925	    for (i = 0; i < length; i++)
926	    {
927	        unsigned bit = (symbol >> i) & 1;
928	        if (*bp == 0)
929	            ZOPFLI_APPEND_DATA(0, out, outsize);
930	        (*out)[*outsize - 1] |= bit << *bp;
931	        *bp = (*bp + 1) & 7;
932	    }
933	}

content.text:
AddSorted verified successfully.

I added a CBMC contract to `AddSorted` in `/app/Syzygy_Zopfli/c_code/zopfli.c:5090`, modeled on the already-verified `AddBit`/`AddBits` functions in the same file (which use the identical `ZOPFLI_APPEND_DATA` append pattern):

- **`requires`** that `out` and `outsize` are valid (fresh) pointers.
- **`requires`** `*outsize` to be non-zero and non-power-of-two, which keeps the `ZOPFLI_APPEND_DATA` macro on its no-reallocation code path (the macro only reallocs when `*outsize` is a power of two).
- **`requires`** `*out` to be a fresh buffer of `(*outsize + 1)` `size_t` elements — the live elements plus room for the one appended value, making all the bubble-down array accesses memory-safe.
- **`assigns`** `*outsize` and the whole `*out` buffer.
- **`ensures`** `*outsize == old(*outsize) + 1` — exactly one element is inserted.

CBMC verifies this cleanly (exit 0). The contract proves memory safety of the append-and-bubble-insert logic and the precise size effect.
